title: Bose-Einstein thermal operators for semidefinite optimization
authors: Michele Minervini, Nana Liu, Mark M. Wilde
abstract: We establish that semidefinite programs (SDPs) over the unbounded positive semidefinite cone are mathematically equivalent to thermodynamic systems of independent bosonic modes: the eigenvalues of the optimization variable play the role of expected occupation numbers, the linear objective plays the role of total expected energy, and the linear equality constraints play the role of conserved non-commuting charges. Building on this perspective, we recast general SDPs as bosonic free-energy minimization problems at strictly positive temperature, regularized by the Bose-Einstein entropy; the original SDP is recovered in the zero-temperature limit. The optimal primal variable takes the form of a Bose-Einstein thermal operator parametrized by the dual variables. We prove an approximation-error bound that depends on the ground-space degeneracy and the spectral gap of the dual slack operator, improving on the linear-in-dimension worst-case duality gap of interior-point methods. We also introduce the Bose-Einstein quantum relative entropy as a Bregman divergence on the unbounded positive semidefinite cone, generated by the negative Bose-Einstein entropy. We propose it as a natural divergence for unnormalized positive operators, for which the standard Umegaki relative entropy can become negative, and we show that it satisfies a restricted monotonicity property under affine maps modeling bosonic Gaussian channels. Finally, we develop hybrid quantum-classical algorithms for the regularized SDP using only Hamiltonian simulation, Hadamard tests, and classical sampling, and bound their runtime in closed form. Unlike existing quantum SDP solvers, whose runtimes scale polynomially with an a priori upper bound on the primal trace, our framework operates directly on the unbounded cone, replacing this bound with a dependence on the spectral structure of the dual slack operator.
summary: The paper introduces a framework for solving semidefinite programs by recasting them as Bose-Einstein free-energy minimization problems. By leveraging the spectral properties of the dual operator, it provides quantum algorithms that scale much more efficiently than classical methods in the presence of a spectral gap. This approach also introduces a new quantum relative entropy specifically designed for unbounded positive operators.
main_problem: Efficiently solving semidefinite programs (SDPs) over the unbounded positive semidefinite cone, which is challenging for classical and existing quantum solvers due to exponential scaling and the lack of an upper bound on the primal trace.
main_result: Established a mathematical equivalence between SDPs and bosonic thermodynamic systems, providing a regularized framework that achieves an exponential advantage in error bounds when the dual slack operator has a spectral gap.
method: A quantum-classical hybrid algorithm using Hamiltonian simulation, Hadamard tests, and Cauchy sampling to perform stochastic gradient and Newton methods on a regularized Bose-Einstein free-energy minimization problem.
model_or_system: A thermodynamic system of independent, non-interacting bosonic modes where the optimization variable represents expected occupation numbers and the dual slack operator acts as a grand canonical Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Expected occupation numbers, total expected energy, thermal expectation values, and the Bose-Einstein quantum relative entropy.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), chemical potential (mu), spectral gap (Delta), ground-space degeneracy (d0), and the minimum eigenvalue of the dual slack operator (lambda_min).
main_assumptions: The dual slack operator is strictly positive definite (lambda_min > 0) and the problem satisfies Slater's condition (strict feasibility).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the equivalence between SDPs and bosonic thermodynamics, then introduces a regularized free-energy minimization approach. It develops the information geometry of a new Bose-Einstein relative entropy, derives the complexity of hybrid quantum algorithms for gradient and Hessian estimation, and concludes with error analysis and contraction properties under affine maps.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to map optimization problems to many-body physics, specifically using bosonic thermal states to bypass the dimension-dependent bottlenecks of classical SDP solvers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 3: The probability density function of the Cauchy distribution p(t; τ) for varying scale parameters τ = m/T. As the series index m increases or temperature T decreases, the distribution flattens, increasing the probability of sampling large evolution times t and necessitating the maximum simulation cutoff t(m) max.
